sourceMovie.title: Blow
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Blow (2001) stuck because it commits to its crime premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. Ted Demme's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The crime and drama blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Requiem for a Dream
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Blow clicked for you, Requiem for a Dream (2000) is a strong next stop: both trade in crime and drama, with momentum and clean stakes. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Blow clicked for you, Casino (1995) is a strong next stop: both trade in crime and drama, with momentum and clean stakes. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Blow?
faq.answer: Try Requiem for a Dream, Casino, American Gangster—each shares DNA with Blow while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Blow?
faq.answer: Blow (2001) is best described as Crime, Drama. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Blow worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.4/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this crime lane.
